content.text: Voice UX turns a text-based agent into a spoken conversational interface. The pipeline has three stages: speech-to-text (STT) converts the user's recorded audio to a transcript, Claude generates a reply, and text-to-speech (TTS) plays the reply back. Each stage has latency, content, and UX constraints that differ from the text chat path. This section covers the full implementation: browser recording, Whisper STT, Claude with a voice-optimised system prompt, OpenAI TTS streaming, barge-in interruption, and the conversation state machine that ties it all together.

attrs.alt: Voice UX pipeline: Microphone -> VAD -> Whisper STT -> Claude -> TTS Speaker, with conversation state machine and target latencies

content.text: OpenAI Whisper (whisper-1) is a reliable STT model that handles accents, background noise, and mixed-language input well. The server receives a WebM audio blob from the browser, writes it to a temp file, and transcribes it before passing the text to Claude.

from openai import AsyncOpenAI
from fastapi import UploadFile
import tempfile, os

openai = AsyncOpenAI()

async def transcribe(audio_file: UploadFile) -> str:
    """Convert uploaded audio to text using Whisper."""
    raw = await audio_file.read()
    suffix = ".webm"  # MediaRecorder default; change if accepting mp3/wav
    with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(suffix=suffix, delete=False) as tmp:
        tmp.write(raw)
        path = tmp.name
    try:
        with open(path, "rb") as f:
            result = await openai.audio.transcriptions.create(
                model="whisper-1",
                file=f,
                response_format="text",
                language="en",   # omit for auto-detection
            )
        return result.strip()
    finally:
        os.unlink(path)

OpenAI tts-1 generates MP3 audio from text. Stream the response as chunked transfer encoding so the browser can start buffering audio before the full file is ready. For higher audio quality at the cost of higher latency, use tts-1-hd.

from openai import AsyncOpenAI
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse

openai = AsyncOpenAI()

async def text_to_speech_stream(text: str, voice: str = "alloy"):
    """Stream TTS audio from OpenAI. Yields raw MP3 chunks."""
    response = await openai.audio.speech.create(
        model="tts-1",          # tts-1-hd for higher quality (slower)
        voice=voice,            # alloy | echo | fable | onyx | nova | shimmer
        input=text,
        response_format="mp3",
    )
    async for chunk in response.iter_bytes(chunk_size=4096):
        yield chunk

@app.post("/tts")
async def tts_endpoint(body: dict):
    text = body.get("text", "")
    voice = body.get("voice", "alloy")
    if not text:
        return {"error": "No text provided"}, 400
    return StreamingResponse(
        text_to_speech_stream(text, voice),
        media_type="audio/mpeg",
        headers={"Transfer-Encoding": "chunked"},
    )

A voice session moves through five states: idle, listening, transcribing, thinking, and speaking. Track this state server-side so the frontend can show the correct visual indicator and the backend can reject duplicate requests.

from enum import Enum
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from anthropic import AsyncAnthropic
import asyncio

client = AsyncAnthropic()

class VoiceState(Enum):
    IDLE          = "idle"
    LISTENING     = "listening"
    TRANSCRIBING  = "transcribing"
    THINKING      = "thinking"
    SPEAKING      = "speaking"

@dataclass
class VoiceSession:
    session_id: str
    history: list = field(default_factory=list)
    state: VoiceState = VoiceState.IDLE
    abort_event: asyncio.Event = field(default_factory=asyncio.Event)

async def voice_turn(session: VoiceSession, audio_bytes: bytes) -> str:
    """Process one voice turn: STT -> Claude -> return text for TTS."""
    session.state = VoiceState.TRANSCRIBING
    session.abort_event.clear()

    # STT
    import tempfile
    with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(suffix=".webm", delete=False) as f:
        f.write(audio_bytes)
        path = f.name
    from openai import AsyncOpenAI
    oai = AsyncOpenAI()
    with open(path, "rb") as f:
        transcript = (await oai.audio.transcriptions.create(
            model="whisper-1", file=f, response_format="text"
        )).strip()

    session.history.append({"role": "user", "content": transcript})
    session.state = VoiceState.THINKING

    # Claude
    response = await client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=512,          # shorter for voice; users prefer brief replies
        system=(
            "You are a voice assistant. Keep responses under 3 sentences. "
            "Do not use markdown, bullet points, or headers — plain spoken language only."
        ),
        messages=session.history,
    )
    reply = response.content[0].text
    session.history.append({"role": "assistant", "content": reply})
    session.state = VoiceState.SPEAKING
    return reply

Hold-to-talk is the most reliable voice input pattern for web apps — it avoids false triggers from ambient audio and requires no VAD tuning. The component sends audio to the server, polls for transcript and reply, then plays TTS audio in sequence.
Decode the streamed MP3 using the Web Audio API for precise control over playback start and end events. This lets the app transition state correctly when speaking finishes.

Barge-in lets the user speak while the assistant is still playing audio, stopping playback immediately. Implement it by monitoring microphone volume during TTS playback and cutting audio when the RMS crosses a threshold.
Claude's default responses use markdown, lists, and parenthetical asides that sound awkward when read aloud. A voice-specific system prompt constrains replies to brief, flowing spoken English.

# System prompt for voice-optimised responses
VOICE_SYSTEM = """
You are a voice assistant. Follow these rules for every response:
- Keep answers under 3 sentences unless the user asks for more detail.
- Use plain spoken English only. No markdown, no bullet points, no headers.
- Say numbers as words: "twelve" not "12", "twenty-five percent" not "25%".
- Avoid parenthetical asides; say them inline or omit them.
- End questions with a rising inflection cue: "... right?" or "... does that help?"
"""

# Example: direct voice turn endpoint
from fastapi import FastAPI, UploadFile
from anthropic import AsyncAnthropic

app = FastAPI()
client = AsyncAnthropic()

@app.post("/voice/{session_id}/turn")
async def voice_turn_endpoint(session_id: str, audio: UploadFile):
    transcript = await transcribe(audio)
    history = voice_sessions.setdefault(session_id, [])
    history.append({"role": "user", "content": transcript})

    response = await client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=256,
        system=VOICE_SYSTEM,
        messages=history,
    )
    reply = response.content[0].text
    history.append({"role": "assistant", "content": reply})
    return {"transcript": transcript, "reply": reply}
More reliable than push-to-talk toggle or VAD
Handles accents, noise; auto-detect language if omitting lang param
tts-1 for speed; tts-1-hd for quality
Strip all markdown from TTS input
RMS threshold on mic during playback
Users need feedback during STT and TTS latency
If Claude generates any markdown despite the system prompt, strip it before passing the text to TTS to avoid the model saying 'asterisk asterisk bold asterisk asterisk'.

import re

def strip_markdown(text: str) -> str:
    """Remove markdown syntax that would sound odd when read aloud."""
    # Headers
    text = re.sub(r"^#{1,6}\s+", "", text, flags=re.MULTILINE)
    # Bold / italic
    text = re.sub(r"\*{1,3}(.+?)\*{1,3}", r"\1", text)
    text = re.sub(r"_{1,3}(.+?)_{1,3}", r"\1", text)
    # Inline code
    text = re.sub(r"`{1,3}(.+?)`{1,3}", r"\1", text, flags=re.DOTALL)
    # Bullet / numbered lists -> natural language
    text = re.sub(r"^\s*[-*+]\s+", "", text, flags=re.MULTILINE)
    text = re.sub(r"^\s*\d+\.\s+", "", text, flags=re.MULTILINE)
    # Links: keep link text, drop URL
    text = re.sub(r"\[(.+?)\]\(.+?\)", r"\1", text)
    # Horizontal rules
    text = re.sub(r"^[-*_]{3,}$", "", text, flags=re.MULTILINE)
    return text.strip()

import pytest
from httpx import AsyncClient, ASGITransport
from app import app, strip_markdown

def test_strip_markdown():
    raw = "**Bold** text and `code` and [link](https://example.com)"
    assert strip_markdown(raw) == "Bold text and code and link"

def test_strip_markdown_headers():
    raw = "## Section\nsome content"
    assert "##" not in strip_markdown(raw)

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_tts_endpoint_returns_audio():
    async with AsyncClient(transport=ASGITransport(app=app), base_url="http://test") as ac:
        resp = await ac.post("/tts", json={"text": "Hello, how can I help?"})
    assert resp.status_code == 200
    assert resp.headers["content-type"] == "audio/mpeg"
    assert len(resp.content) > 0

A voice UX layer adds STT, TTS, and a conversation state machine on top of the standard text agent. The critical design choices are: use hold-to-talk to avoid VAD complexity, constrain Claude's replies to spoken English via the system prompt, strip residual markdown before TTS, and implement barge-in with an RMS threshold so users can interrupt naturally. Target a total turn latency under 4 seconds to maintain a conversational feel.
